generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance MotorActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Permanent magnet synchronous motor component of MABI MAX 100 industrial robot providing rotational actuation with integrated position encoders for kinematic tracking"
]

instance GearboxActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Cycloidal gear transmission system integrated with the MABI MAX 100 robot's motor assembly, enabling precise torque conversion and mechanical advantage for industrial applications"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance MotorPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Primary position encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft of MABI MAX 100 providing high-resolution rotational position data for real-time kinematic tracking"
    DTDFVocab:producedData MotorPositionDataTransmitted
]

instance GearboxPositionEncoder : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary position encoder positioned behind the gearbox of MABI MAX 100 providing precise kinematic feedback for gear ratio verification and mechanical alignment monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData GearboxPositionDataTransmitted
]

instance TorqueSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded torque sensor in MABI MAX 100's drive axis measuring rotational torque for friction analysis and load monitoring during dynamic operations"
    DTDFVocab:producedData TorqueDataTransmitted
]

instance VibrationSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Vibration sensor integrated into MABI MAX 100's mechanical structure monitoring mechanical health through frequency analysis of structural vibrations"
    DTDFVocab:producedData VibrationDataTransmitted
]

instance TemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded temperature sensor in MABI MAX 100's motor-gearbox assembly monitoring thermal conditions to prevent overheating during high-load operations"
    DTDFVocab:producedData TemperatureDataTransmitted
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance MotorPositionDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom MotorPositionEncoder
]

instance GearboxPositionDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom GearboxPositionEncoder
]

instance TorqueDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TorqueSensor
]

instance VibrationDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom VibrationSensor
]

instance TemperatureDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance SysMLFunctionalArchitectureModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML-based functional architecture model of MABI MAX 100's motor-gearbox-sensor topology describing component-interconnection structure and behavioral interfaces"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler
]

instance SysMLStateMachineModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML state machine model (v1.7) representing MABI MAX 100's operational states and transitions for behavioral specification and simulation validation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLModelingEnabler
]

instance MontiArcAutomatonModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MontiArc automaton model capturing detailed behavioral specification of MABI MAX 100's dynamic responses under varying workload conditions"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MontiArcSimulationEnabler
]

instance SimulinkFrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink model for friction analysis of MABI MAX 100's drive system incorporating real-time sensor data for predictive maintenance"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkSimulationEnabler
]

instance RawSensorData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated raw sensor data streams from MABI MAX 100's position encoders, torque sensors, vibration sensors, and temperature sensors"
    DTDFVocab:fromData MotorPositionDataTransmitted, GearboxPositionDataTransmitted, TorqueDataTransmitted, VibrationDataTransmitted, TemperatureDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance AbstractedFrictionData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed and abstracted friction data derived from raw sensor measurements enabling higher-level analysis of mechanical interactions in MABI MAX 100's drive system"
    DTDFVocab:fromData RawSensorData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FrictionAnalysisEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SysMLModelingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML-based modeling toolchain enabling creation and validation of functional architecture and behavioral models for MABI MAX 100's digital twin"
    DTDFVocab:enables FunctionalArchitectureService, BehavioralSimulationService
]

instance MontiArcSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc simulation framework supporting detailed behavioral analysis of MABI MAX 100's dynamic responses through automaton-based modeling"
    DTDFVocab:enables BehavioralSimulationService
]

instance SimulinkSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for dynamic system simulation of MABI MAX 100's friction models and real-time data processing"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionAnalysisService
]

instance DataAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Passive data aggregation pipeline implementing one-way data flow from MABI MAX 100's sensors to digital twin storage and processing systems"
    DTDFVocab:enables DataAggregationService
]

instance FrictionAnalysisEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Abstraction and analysis framework transforming raw sensor data into friction models for predictive maintenance applications"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionAnalysisService
]

// C6: Services
instance DataAggregationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Passive data aggregation service collecting and organizing real-time contextual data traces from MABI MAX 100's embedded sensors"
    DTDFVocab:provides RawSensorDataInsight
]

instance FunctionalArchitectureService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "SysML-based functional architecture service modeling the component-interconnection structure of MABI MAX 100's motor-gearbox-sensor system"
    DTDFVocab:provides ArchitectureValidationInsight
]

instance BehavioralSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Behavioral simulation service validating MABI MAX 100's operational states and dynamic responses through SysML state machines and MontiArc automata"
    DTDFVocab:provides StateValidationInsight
]

instance FrictionAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Friction analysis service processing sensor data to identify and model mechanical friction patterns in MABI MAX 100's drive system"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionDiagnosisInsight, PredictiveMaintenanceAction
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance RawSensorDataInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight providing real-time access to aggregated raw sensor data from MABI MAX 100's position encoders, torque sensors, and temperature sensors"
]

instance ArchitectureValidationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Validation insight confirming the structural and behavioral consistency of MABI MAX 100's SysML-based functional architecture model"
]

instance StateValidationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Behavioral validation insight verifying the correctness of MABI MAX 100's operational state transitions through simulation testing"
]

instance FrictionDiagnosisInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Diagnostic insight identifying friction patterns and anomalies in MABI MAX 100's drive system based on processed sensor data"
]

instance PredictiveMaintenanceAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Predictive maintenance action recommendation system suggesting maintenance interventions based on friction analysis and operational workload patterns"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingAutomationEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Operational environment for MABI MAX 100 industrial robot within manufacturing automation domain requiring high-precision motion control and predictive maintenance"
    base:contains MABIMAX100RobotSystem
]

// C5: Components
instance MABIMAX100RobotSystem : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "MABI MAX 100 industrial robot comprising motor-actuator assembly, cycloidal gear transmission, and embedded sensor network for real-time monitoring"
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingAutomationEnvironment
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the described Digital Twin system is **not explicitly detailed** in the provided documents. However, based on the context of DTs as active components [35–37], the following **hypothetical technical characteristics** (inferred from broader DT literature and related concepts) are implied:

**Control Flows:**
- **Command types:** Likely include **start/stop**, **parameter adjustments** (e.g., speed, temperature), **maintenance triggers**, and **emergency shutdowns**.
- **Control message formats:** Standardized formats (e.g., **JSON**, **XML**, or **protocol buffers**) for interoperability with industrial control systems (e.g., PLCs, SCADA).

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms:**
- **Not explicitly mentioned** in the documents, but industry standards (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT with TLS**) are commonly used for secure command execution.
- **Role-based access control (RBAC)** for authorization (implied by IoT platform security models like **ThingsBoard** [53]).

**Feedback Systems:**
- **Closed-loop mechanisms** (e.g., **PID controllers**, **adaptive algorithms**) to validate command execution and adjust physical behavior dynamically.
- **Confirmation protocols** (e.g., **acknowledgment messages**) to ensure command receipt and execution (e.g., via **MQTT QoS levels**).

**Conflict Resolution:**
- **Priority-based scheduling** (e.g., emergency commands overriding routine operations).
- **Conflict detection algorithms** (e.g., **temporal logic** or **finite-state machines**) to prevent contradictory commands.

**Command Execution Confirmation:**
- **Event-driven feedback** (e.g., **status updates**, **telemetry responses**) to confirm physical system state changes.
- **Logging and auditing** (e.g., via **ThingsBoard** [53] or **Eclipse Layout Kernel** [55] for traceability).

**Documentation Gap:**
The provided documents **do not specify** virtual-to-physical interaction details, focusing instead on **digital shadow** (one-way) data flows. For comprehensive DT control, refer to broader Industry 4.0 standards (e.g., **OPC UA**, **ISO/IEC 42010**).
"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system described in the documents emphasizes **temporal synchronization** through **data trace aggregation and abstraction**, where data traces are collected at varying granularities depending on the use case (e.g., manufacturing processes like injection molding). The **latency requirements** are implicitly tied to the **processing component’s computational steps**, which consume input data traces and produce output traces for the Digital Shadow. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms) are not provided, the **traceability** of data traces—originating from associations to processing components—implies real-time or near-real-time processing for maintaining consistency between physical and digital representations. The **time scales** for services are governed by the **processing logic** (e.g., simulation or data calculation models) and their integration with tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation methods, which may introduce variable temporal resolutions based on model complexity. No specific latency thresholds (e.g., <100ms) or fixed sampling intervals are mentioned in the documents."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **orchestration and coordination** of the Digital Twin system is framed within the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**, which defines a **processing-centric architecture** where data traces are aggregated, abstracted, and processed by components encoding logic in **simulation or data calculation models**. The **architecture patterns** are derived from **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL or MontiArc) and their integration with tools like **Simulink** or general-purpose languages (C++, Java). The **resource management** is implicit in the **processing component’s** role, which consumes input traces and produces outputs, ensuring traceability via transitive associations. However, no explicit **orchestration frameworks** (e.g., Kubernetes, microservices) or **system-wide coordination mechanisms** (e.g., event-driven architectures, API gateways) are mentioned. The **concretization** of the reference model allows for customization, but the **component integration** is primarily tied to **heterogeneous modeling notations** and tools, lacking detailed specifications for inter-component communication protocols."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. These layers are managed via methodologies such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Methodology)**, **SPES (Systematic Process for Engineering Systems)**, and **SMArDT (Smart Digital Twin Methodology)**. Transition management between phases is facilitated by **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL (Avionics Architecture Description Language), MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition rules. Behavior modeling integrates **state machines (SysML v1.7), MontiArc Automaton, and simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++/Java**). Lifecycle governance involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-tool interoperability**, ensuring alignment across modeling notations and tools. The lifecycle is iterative, with **abstraction refinement** from high-level requirements to low-level physical representations, governed by **MBSE frameworks** to maintain consistency and traceability.

Digital Shadows, as referenced in **Kritzinger et al. [32]**, operate as a subset of DTs with a **one-way data flow** from physical objects to digital representations, lacking autonomous operation or feedback mechanisms. Their lifecycle is purpose-driven, focusing on **contextual data aggregation and abstraction** for specific applications (e.g., manufacturing), often integrated into DT systems for enhanced monitoring and analytics.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system addresses **fidelity and validity** through **multi-layered MBSE abstraction** (requirements → functional → logical → physical), ensuring hierarchical consistency. **Positional uncertainty** is mitigated via the **dual-encoder arrangement**, reducing backlash errors in gear transmissions. While explicit **validation and verification (V&V) methods** (e.g., formal testing, uncertainty quantification) are not detailed, the **levels of abstraction** in MBSE implicitly support **model fidelity** by decoupling logical behaviors (e.g., SysML state machines) from physical implementations (e.g., Simulink simulations). **Quality assurance** is inferred through the integration of **heterogeneous tools** (SysML, Simulink, MQTT) and **contextual data aggregation**, though specific error detection/correction mechanisms or uncertainty quantification frameworks (e.g., probabilistic models) are not described in the provided documents."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The **technical network infrastructure** for this system is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the digital shadow’s **communication flow** is defined as a **one-way data stream** (Kritzinger et al., [32]), implying reliance on **lightweight, scalable protocols** (e.g., MQTT, as referenced in [52] and [53]) for data transmission. No specifics regarding **network topology** (e.g., star, mesh), **bandwidth/latency requirements**, **reliability mechanisms** (e.g., redundancy), or **security protocols** (e.g., TLS, encryption) are mentioned. The system’s architecture does not explicitly describe **control flows** or bidirectional communication, aligning with the passive nature of digital shadows. Tools like Mosquitto, ThingsBoard, or OPC UA are not referenced in the context of this system’s network infrastructure."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for this system is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. The digital shadow is described as a **purpose-driven abstraction** of physical data (Reichenstein et al., [56]), but no mention of **cloud/edge/on-premise deployment**, **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms**, or **availability/reliability assurances** (e.g., Kubernetes, Docker, Raspberry Pi) is provided. The system’s deployment model is not specified beyond its role as a **passive data aggregator**, and no references to frameworks (e.g., Angular, PostgreSQL) or tools (e.g., Simulink, SysML) are tied to deployment infrastructure in the given context."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system integrates horizontally via **MQTT** for streaming sensor data (e.g., time series from the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller or robot components) and **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** for standardized asset representation. While the documents mention **interoperability with external systems** (e.g., Siemens controllers), they do not specify **enterprise-level integrations** (e.g., ERP/MES) or **OPC UA** as a protocol. The system’s design prioritizes **purpose-driven data aggregation** from the physical asset’s environment, with no explicit mention of cross-system coordination or APIs for external Digital Twins. Integration patterns are tool-specific (e.g., Simulink, SysML) and aligned with the **concretized Digital Shadow Reference Model**, ensuring traceability to source data and processing components."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry standards** primarily through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks and **architecture description languages (ADLs)**. Key standards include:
- **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for **functional/logical architecture** and **behavioral modeling** (state machines, IBDs).
- **AADL (Avionics Architecture Description Language)** for **component-connector modeling** and **real-time systems**.
- **MontiArc** for **automaton-based behavior** and **architecture composition**.
- **ACME (Architecture Description Language)** for **generic system structuring**.
Interoperability is achieved via **multi-tool integration** (e.g., **Simulink, C++/Java**) and **heterogeneous stakeholder alignment**, though no explicit **ISO/IEC standards (e.g., ISO 23247 for DTs)** or **OPC UA/ROS** protocols are mentioned. **Certification requirements** are not detailed, but compliance is implied through **ADL-based validation** and **simulation-based testing**. The system follows **MBSE governance** for **traceability and consistency**, ensuring adherence to **engineering best practices** rather than formal certification.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents incorporates **safety-critical constraints** through **physical boundaries** of robotic assets, such as the **MABI robot** (Document [44]), where the **safety cell restricts the maximum working range to [-90°°, 90°°]**, ensuring collision avoidance and operator safety. No explicit **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., TLS, OAuth, or encryption standards) are documented for data transmission, though **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is referenced (Document [52]) as a communication protocol for IoT data exchange. While MQTT itself does not inherently enforce security, its use implies reliance on **external security configurations** (e.g., TLS for secure MQTT over WebSockets) not detailed in the documents.

The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (Documents [46], [47], [50]) provides a **semantic structure** for asset metadata, including **safety-related annotations** (e.g., operational limits, hazard classifications), but does not explicitly define **access control mechanisms** or **privacy-preserving data governance**. No references to **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **compliance frameworks** (e.g., GDPR, ISO/IEC 27001) are present in the provided materials. **Risk assessment** is implied through **physical safety constraints** but lacks formalized **cybersecurity risk mitigation strategies** or **fail-safe mechanisms** beyond hardware-level safeguards."
]
